content.text: Python decorators are higher-order functions that wrap other callables to add cross-cutting behavior — logging, timing, authentication, retries, schema validation, and tool registration — without modifying the wrapped function's source. In agentic AI systems they are the canonical way to attach policy uniformly across every tool a model may invoke.
is a callable that accepts a function (or class) and returns a replacement. The
statement is shorthand for passing the function through that callable at definition time: writing

Decorators are a first-class Python language feature (PEP 318, Python 2.4) based on the observation that functions are objects. Because Python evaluates the decorator expression and calls it with the function at import time, a decorator can replace, augment, or register the function before any call site ever sees it. The result stored back under the original name is whatever the decorator returns — usually a wrapper function, but sometimes a class instance or the original function unchanged (for pure registration).
Parameterized decorators add another level: a

is called with arguments and returns the real decorator. The three-level nesting — factory → decorator → wrapper — is the dominant pattern in production code and is what frameworks like FastAPI and LangChain use internally.

Agent systems expose dozens or hundreds of tools to a language model. Every tool may need identical cross-cutting concerns: a trace span for observability, a retry loop for transient API failures, input validation against a JSON schema, and registration into the dispatcher's tool table. Without decorators, each tool author must copy the same boilerplate — which diverges, contains bugs, and clutters the domain logic.
Decorators solve this by letting the cross-cutting concern be authored once and applied to many functions with a single line. They also make the concern
immediately knows the function is traced and retried, without reading any call-site code. This is the Decorator pattern from the Gang-of-Four design patterns book, realized as syntax.
decorator is how functions are registered into the model's tool schema. LangChain's
, Anthropic's tool-use JSON schema helpers, and FastAPI's
all use this pattern. Understanding the mechanics lets you write your own registration and policy decorators rather than depending on a framework.
— write retry, logging, and validation once; apply to every tool with a single annotation. Any fix to the shared decorator propagates everywhere instantly.
above a function is a declaration of intent, visible without reading the body or the call sites.
— a decorator can add the function to a registry dict at import time while still returning a callable wrapper, combining registration and behavior in one step.
applies three independent concerns whose wrappers layer cleanly around the core function, each unaware of the others.
— because decorators are ordinary callables, they can be tested independently by calling them with a mock function, and decorated functions can be unwrapped with
— a reader who skips the decorator stack and reads only the function body may not realize the function is retried or validated. Documentation and naming discipline matter.
, all wrappers appear as the name
in tracebacks. Even with wraps, a deep decorator stack adds frames that must be mentally filtered.
— stacking too many decorators obscures the actual execution path and makes it hard to reason about which decorator is responsible for an observed behavior.

— decorators run at import time, not at call time. A decorator that opens a database connection or reads a config file will do so the moment the module is imported, which can break test isolation if not handled carefully.
— decorators applied to methods must account for the descriptor protocol.
interact with custom wrappers in non-obvious ways; testing is required.
The Python interpreter processes decorators during module loading. When it encounters:

The decorator must accept a callable and return a callable. The returned object is bound to the name
in the local scope — which is why
from the original function onto the wrapper, preserving introspection.
When decorators are stacked, Python applies them bottom-up: the innermost decorator (closest to
) wraps the function first, then each outer decorator wraps that result. At call time the outermost wrapper runs first — so a stack of
executes: trace → retry → validate → fn → validate-return → retry-return → trace-return.
Most decorators work through closures: the wrapper function captures the original

in its enclosing scope. Each call to the wrapper can inspect or modify arguments, call the original, and then inspect or modify the return value. The original function remains accessible as the closure variable and is never altered in memory.

A decorator factory is a function that returns a decorator. The three-level nesting is: the factory is called with configuration arguments, returns a decorator, the decorator is called with the function, and the decorator returns the wrapper. Each level is a distinct function object capturing variables from the outer scope.
The diagram below shows how a stack of three decorators wraps a core tool function and how the call and return paths travel through the layers:
The most important architectural detail is that stacking is applied

. A decorator that must see the result of all validation (e.g. a tracing decorator) should be the outermost one — listed topmost in the stack syntax. A decorator that guards entry (e.g. rate limiting) should be outermost too, so it can short-circuit before any inner logic runs.
— logging, timing, retries, auth checks, rate limiting, and input validation that must be applied uniformly to many functions.
— adding a function to a registry, tool table, or route map at import time.
are standard library decorators. Custom caching decorators for agent tools can key on serialized arguments and store results in Redis.
— FastAPI routes, Django views, Celery tasks, and LangChain tools are all registered via decorators because it keeps the declaration co-located with the function.
The behavior changes the core semantics of the function (return type, exceptions raised) in a way that is not obvious from the decorator name. Use an explicit wrapper call instead.
The concern applies to only one function and is tightly coupled to its logic — inline it.
Debugging a complex agent pipeline and you need to trace execution: a decorator-heavy stack makes step-through debugging harder; explicit middleware with named stages is clearer.
Step 1 — Write a basic decorator with functools.wraps

import time
from functools import wraps


def log_latency(fn):
    """Log the wall-clock time of every call to fn."""
    @wraps(fn)
    def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
        start = time.perf_counter()
        result = fn(*args, **kwargs)
        elapsed = time.perf_counter() - start
        print(f"{fn.__name__} finished in {elapsed:.3f}s")
        return result
    return wrapper


@log_latency
def fetch_context(query: str) -> list[str]:
    # simulate retrieval
    time.sleep(0.05)
    return [f"doc about {query}"]


# fetch_context.__name__ == "fetch_context"  (preserved by wraps)
results = fetch_context("vector stores")
# Output: fetch_context finished in 0.050s
Step 2 — Write a parameterized decorator factory
A class-based decorator is useful when the wrapper needs to maintain state between calls. Implementing
Example 2 — Realistic: combined tracing + schema validation for agent tools

"""
Production pattern used in agentic systems:
- Validate tool arguments against a Pydantic model before calling the function.
- Emit an OpenTelemetry span wrapping the call.
- Raise a descriptive error if arguments are invalid.
"""
import time
from functools import wraps
from typing import TypeVar, get_type_hints
from pydantic import BaseModel, ValidationError

F = TypeVar("F")


def _validate_call(fn, args, kwargs):
    """Validate *kwargs* against fn's first Pydantic-typed argument if present."""
    hints = get_type_hints(fn)
    # Find first Pydantic model in annotations
    for name, hint in hints.items():
        if isinstance(hint, type) and issubclass(hint, BaseModel) and name in kwargs:
            try:
                hint(**kwargs[name]) if isinstance(kwargs[name], dict) else hint.model_validate(kwargs[name])
            except ValidationError as exc:
                raise ValueError(f"Tool {fn.__name__!r} input validation failed: {exc}") from exc


def traced_tool(fn):
    """Wrap fn with OTel span + input validation (simplified, no OTel SDK dep)."""
    @wraps(fn)
    def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
        t0 = time.perf_counter()
        ok = False
        try:
            _validate_call(fn, args, kwargs)
            result = fn(*args, **kwargs)
            ok = True
            return result
        finally:
            elapsed_ms = (time.perf_counter() - t0) * 1000
            status = "ok" if ok else "error"
            print(f"[span] tool={fn.__name__} status={status} latency_ms={elapsed_ms:.1f}")
    return wrapper


class SearchArgs(BaseModel):
    query: str
    top_k: int = 5


@traced_tool
def search(args: SearchArgs) -> list[str]:
    """Search the knowledge base."""
    return [f"result_{i}" for i in range(args.top_k)]


search(args=SearchArgs(query="transformers", top_k=3))
# [span] tool=search status=ok latency_ms=0.1

The following pattern is used in production agentic systems that expose dozens of tools to a model. Each tool is a plain Python function decorated with three layers: registration (so the dispatcher knows about it), rate limiting (so burst calls to external APIs stay within quotas), and tracing (so every call appears in the observability platform). No tool author writes any of this logic — they annotate their function and focus on domain logic.

At scale this pattern supports hundreds of tools with zero boilerplate in each tool function. The rate limiter bucket is shared across all calls to a given tool regardless of which agent thread triggers them, because the closure captures the same
instance at decoration time. This is a deliberate import-time side effect: state is initialized once, not on every call.
Mistake 2 — Calling a factory without parentheses
Mistake 3 — Sharing mutable state across calls unsafely
Mistake 4 — Applying a decorator to a method that alters self
. Introspection tools, documentation generators, and OpenAPI schema builders depend on it.
— a decorator should orchestrate, not implement. Heavy logic (retry backoff strategy, schema validation) belongs in helper functions tested independently of the decorator machinery.
are self-documenting. Avoid generic names like
— separating retry, logging, and validation into three decorators makes each independently testable and reusable. A single decorator that retries, logs, and validates is an antipattern.
— when the order of decorators matters (e.g. tracing must be outermost), add a comment or module-level constant that specifies the canonical order.
does this automatically) so tests can call the raw function without triggering retries or rate limits.
— if a decorator opens a file or connects to a database, that side effect runs at import, which breaks module isolation in unit tests. Defer I/O to first call with lazy initialization.

Each function call through a decorator stack incurs one extra Python frame per decorator layer. For a stack of three decorators, every call goes through four Python frames instead of one. CPython's frame overhead is roughly 0.1–0.5 µs per frame on modern hardware, so a three-layer stack adds under 2 µs to a function call. For tools that call external APIs (network latency 10–1000 ms) or LLMs (100–10 000 ms), decorator overhead is negligible.

stores results keyed on hashable arguments, so repeated identical calls to an embedding or search tool return instantly. At agent scale (thousands of calls per session), cache hit rates of 10–40 % are common for retrieval tools because many agent queries reuse similar sub-queries.

. A rate-limiter bucket or a call-count accumulator inside a decorator closure is shared across all threads calling the decorated function. In high-concurrency agent systems (many parallel tool calls), use thread-safe primitives (

import functools
import time

@functools.lru_cache(maxsize=1024)
def get_embedding(text: str) -> tuple[float, ...]:
    """Expensive call; cached by text content."""
    # result is a tuple (hashable) for LRU cache compatibility
    ...


# Check cache stats
stats = get_embedding.cache_info()
print(stats)
# CacheInfo(hits=340, misses=60, maxsize=1024, currsize=60)
# 85 % cache hit rate on 400 calls
— dispatch decorator: routes calls to different implementations based on the type of the first argument.
— turns a generator into a context manager using a decorator; often confused with function decorators but works on
with exponential backoff, jitter, custom retry conditions, and per-attempt logging.
— correct transparent decorator library that handles descriptors,
, and async functions without special cases. Recommended for library authors writing general-purpose decorators.
for instrumenting functions with distributed trace spans.
(v2) validates all arguments against type annotations using Pydantic models, with full coercion and error reporting.

# pydantic v2 validate_call
from pydantic import validate_call

@validate_call
def chunk_document(text: str, chunk_size: int = 512, overlap: int = 64) -> list[str]:
    """Split text into overlapping chunks."""
    chunks = []
    start = 0
    while start < len(text):
        chunks.append(text[start:start + chunk_size])
        start += chunk_size - overlap
    return chunks

chunk_document("hello world", chunk_size="bad")  # raises ValidationError, not TypeError
Decorators are one of several mechanisms for applying cross-cutting behavior. The table below compares them along the axes that matter most for agent tool systems:
OOP hierarchies; not for standalone functions
Decorators that enforce security policy (authentication, authorization, rate limiting) must be applied consistently and must not be accidentally skipped. The main failure mode is
: a new tool function is added without the required
— the tool registration decorator can check for required decorators by inspecting
for sentinel attributes set by mandatory decorators. If a mandatory decorator is missing, raise at import time, not at call time.
— instead of relying on every author to annotate correctly, require tools to subclass a base that applies security wrappers in
— a validation decorator should run before any network call or file access, preventing injection via malformed inputs. Validate against strict Pydantic schemas, not just Python type hints.
— a security decorator that swallows exceptions can hide failures. Always re-raise after logging, or raise a documented exception type.

— decorators that capture API keys or credentials in closures keep them in memory for the lifetime of the function object. Avoid capturing secrets in long-lived module-level closures; prefer reading from environment variables at call time.

Think of a decorator as an onion wrapper around a function. The function is the core. Each decorator adds a layer. When a caller dials in, they punch through every layer in sequence (outermost first) before reaching the core. The result bubbles back out through every layer on the way home.

syntax is just a visual reminder that the layers are applied at definition time, not at call time — the onion is assembled once, then used many times. Adding a new layer (a new decorator) does not change the interface callers see; they still call the outer surface with the same arguments and get the same type of result.
a function that builds a better version of another function
— same interface, extended behavior, assembled once at module load time.
evaluated at import time — the name in scope is always the wrapper, not the original.
inside every wrapper function to preserve
— omitting it silently breaks introspection, docstring access, and schema generators.
Parameterized decorators require a three-level nesting: factory(config) → decorator(fn) → wrapper(*args). The factory returns the decorator, the decorator returns the wrapper.
Stacking is applied inside-out at definition time but executes outside-in at call time — the topmost decorator in source is the outermost wrapper at runtime and runs first.

Use decorators for cross-cutting concerns (logging, retry, validation, registration) that must be applied consistently to many functions. Avoid them when behavior is tightly coupled to a single function.
Shared mutable state in a decorator closure (rate limiter buckets, call counters) is shared across all calls including concurrent ones — protect with
for library-grade transparent decorators that correctly handle methods and async functions.
Security-critical decorators (auth, rate limiting) should be enforced structurally — use sentinel attributes and registration-time checks to catch missing decorators at import time, not at call time.
